In C# programming, constructors are special methods automatically called when an object is created from a class. They initialize the object‚Äôs initial state (e.g., setting property values). A constructor has the same name as the class and does not have a return type (not even `void`). This JSON file explains the types of constructors (default, parameterized, static), their usage, and examples in a simple way. Code examples are compatible with .NET 8, but due to limited C# support in Pyodide, they should be tested with Visual Studio Code or Visual Studio using .NET SDK. Run the code with the `dotnet run` command. Examples use static values and do not require user input.
A default constructor takes no parameters and sets default values for an object. If no constructor is defined in a class, C# automatically provides a default constructor.

In this code:
- The `Person` class uses a default constructor to set default values for `Name` and `Age`.
- The constructor is automatically called when creating an object (`new Person()`).
- The `Introduce` method prints the properties.
- Note: Run this code with .NET SDK in Visual Studio Code using `dotnet run`.
A parameterized constructor accepts external values and assigns them to the class‚Äôs properties during object creation.

In this code:
- The `Student` class uses a parameterized constructor to accept `Name` and `Grade` values.
- Values are set during object creation.
- The `DisplayInfo` method prints the information.
A static constructor initializes static members of a class (e.g., static fields). It is called automatically once when the class is first used and cannot have parameters.

In this code:
- The `Logger` class uses a static constructor to initialize `LogCount`.
- The static constructor is called automatically when the class is first used.
- The `Log` method increments `LogCount` for each call.
A class can have multiple constructors with different parameters, known as constructor overloading.

In this code:
- The `Car` class defines three constructors: default, one-parameter, and two-parameter.
- Each constructor initializes the object differently.
- The `DisplayInfo` method prints the object‚Äôs information.
Constructors support encapsulation by safely initializing an object‚Äôs state.

In this code:
- The `BankAccount` class initializes the `Balance` property via a constructor.
- The `Balance` property‚Äôs `set` method is private, restricting external changes.
- The `Deposit` method safely increases the balance.

items.description: Initialization: Sets initial values for objects.
Safety: Supports encapsulation to protect data.
Flexibility: Overloading allows multiple initialization options.

content.text: Constructors can present challenges for beginners.

items.description: Complexity: Overloading and static constructors can be confusing.
Error Handling: Incorrect parameter values can cause errors.
Pyodide Limitation: C# code is not fully supported in Pyodide.

content.text:
- **Dangers**: Incorrect parameter validation or `null` values in constructors can cause errors. Excessive overloading can complicate code.
- **Recommendations**: Validate parameters carefully and set default values. Prefer simple constructor designs. Test code with .NET SDK in Visual Studio Code (`dotnet run`). Refer to Microsoft‚Äôs official documentation (https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/csharp/).
This example causes an error due to an invalid parameter value.

In this code:
- The `BankAccount` class does not allow a negative balance.
- The constructor checks `initialBalance` and throws an error for negative values.
- Solution: Validate parameters and use correct values.
